title: [source] A material-type filter matches a log only through quantities it references, usin

given: A log references quantity Q at revision R1.
Quantity Q is later revised to R2 with a different material type.
when: The log list is filtered by Q's material type as recorded at R1.
Then the log matches R1's material type, not the material type currently on Q at R2.

why_non_obvious: The filter is exposed as a pseudo-field on logs, but it does not read a log field; it traverses the log-to-quantity reference and explicitly joins the quantity material type on both target ID and target revision ID.

source_read.rule: A material-type filter matches a log only through quantities it references, using the referenced quantity revision's material type rather than the quantity's current material type.

title: [source] A material quantity may reference multiple material-type taxonomy terms, and ref

given: A material quantity is being recorded.
One or more material-type names or terms are supplied, including a name with no existing taxonomy term.
when: The quantity is saved.

why_non_obvious: A field named singularly as `material_type` and labelled “Material type” can be mistaken for a required single enum-like classification; the implementation makes it a multi-valued entity reference with auto-creation enabled.

source_read.rule: A material quantity may reference multiple material-type taxonomy terms, and references can create missing material-type terms automatically.

title: [source] An empty material-type selection imposes no filtering condition; it does not mea

given: The input-log view has no selected quantity material types.
when: The view query is executed.
Then all otherwise eligible logs remain visible, regardless of whether their referenced quantities have material types.

why_non_obvious: Filter implementations often treat an empty value as a null/absence predicate or as an empty IN set. Here the query handler exits before adding any condition.

source_read.rule: An empty material-type selection imposes no filtering condition; it does not mean that only logs without a material type should be returned.

title: [source] Selecting multiple material types has existential/OR semantics: a log is include

given: A log references two quantities, one with material type A and one with material type B.
The user selects material types A and B.
when: The material-type filter is applied.
Then the log is included once; it need not have every selected type on a single quantity.

why_non_obvious: A naïve materialized join can produce one row per matching quantity and accidentally duplicate logs, while a naïve multi-select implementation may require all selected terms. The source uses an IN membership subquery specifically to avoid duplicates.

source_read.rule: The material-type filter is available only on the farm_log view's page_type and page_asset displays when the view arguments contain input anywhere; it is not a universal log-list filter.

when: the resource is read/written under the eventual, selective-disclosure model
then: the value visible to the actor matches the source's access decision

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this access-check site 'soft': [access-check] The route assumes a server-side `access farm import index` permission check; under eventual consistency, replicated authorization state may temporarily be stale and allow or deny access differently until synchronization, making this an eventual access-snapshot invariant.

title: [cm/revision-lock] baseFieldDefinitions

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] The source assumes the entity is revisionable, which requires preserving revision history but not an exclusive lock; concurrent revisions may temporarily diverge under eventual consistency and converge as history is synchronized.

title: [cm/unique-constraint] baseFieldDefinitions

given: two records that each satisfy a server-side uniqueness invariant in isolation
when: both are created independently (offline / concurrent replicas)
then: the port's convergence rule for the uniqueness violation is observed and asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this unique-constraint site 'hard': [unique-constraint] The `UniqueField` constraint assumes globally unique field values at write time, which eventual consistency cannot guarantee across concurrent replicas without a chosen conflict-resolution or coordination strategy.

title: [cm/revision-lock] setNewRevision

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] The setter records a `new_revision` marker but does not itself enforce an exclusive lock or atomic revision check, so replicas may temporarily disagree while the revision state and history converge.

title: [cm/revision-lock] submitForm

title: [cm/access-check] submitForm

title: [hook-implementation] ViewsExecutionHooks

given: the entity state the hook keys on
when: the lifecycle event that triggers the hook occurs
then: the hook's derived side effect is observable at the value boundary

why_non_obvious: A hook fires a side effect on an entity lifecycle event. A re-implementer who models only the primary write can miss the derived state the hook maintains.
